entries.summary: In a major step toward healing a years-long rift, President Wadagni visited the military-led Sahelian countries, raising prospects for renewed border security cooperation and the reopening of the Benin-Niger border.

Al-Qaeda-affiliated militants continued to attack state forces, particularly in the country’s northern regions, as authorities doubled down on their military-first approach to tackling insecurity and intensified their crackdown on dissent.
Tensions persisted over preparations for the May 2027 presidential election, while restrictions on civic space and economic pressures continued.
President Biya’s prolonged absence during his trip to Switzerland fuelled speculation about his health and succession, while clashes between government forces and Anglophone separatists continued.
The army leadership denied rumours of its involvement in a plot to overthrow President Touadéra, Bangui became the latest African capital to receive migrants deported from the U.S.,

Amid mounting security threats, authorities leaned toward reviving their security partnership with France less than two years after French troops were expelled from Chad; intercommunal violence left several people dead

Authorities unveiled plans for a new electoral framework, drawing an initially positive response from the opposition; Abidjan signalled its openness to reviving security cooperation with its Sahelian neighbours.
Fighting intensified in South Kivu as Kinshasa and allied militias stepped up efforts to recapture territory held by the Rwanda-backed M23 rebel group around Minembwe town
Asmara’s relations with Addis Ababa remained tense while its ties with Cairo continued to deepen; meanwhile, the UN special rapporteur drew attention to the human rights situation in Eritrea.

The ruling party secured a big parliamentary majority, but elections were marred by violence and the exclusion of 46 constituencies from voting, including all of Tigray region; meanwhile, tensions between the federal government and Tigray People’s Liberation Front (TPLF) continued to escalate.
The ruling coalition backing
Protests at a U.S.-backed Ebola facility left several dead, while tensions between the government and the opposition mounted over a controversial finance bill.

Al-Qaeda-affiliated militants sustained pressure on Bamako and key western supply corridors, as government counter-insurgency operations reportedly caused civilian casualties and authorities introduced new measures to contain jihadist violence.

Islamic State-affiliated combatants continued to operate across large swathes of the northern province of Cabo Delgado and at sea; the killing of two opposition members in May continued to fuel political tensions.

Benin President Romuald Wadagni’s visit to Niger marked a significant step toward rapprochement between the two neighbours and increased the prospects for reopening the border; jihadist groups escalated their campaign, launching a series of large-scale attacks, including on Niamey’s airport.
Joint operations by Nigerian and U.S. forces disrupted jihadist networks in the North East, but extremist, criminal and herder-farmer violence persisted across much of the country.
Nile Waters
Nile Waters
Nile Waters
Kigali continued to face scrutiny over its role in the conflict in eastern DR Congo (DRC), even as it pursued diplomatic engagement abroad.
The power struggle at the top of the state continued as the National Assembly passed a constitutional reform curbing presidential powers.
Violence erupted in Mogadishu between federal forces and militias aligned with opposition figures just weeks after President Mohamud’s original four-year term expired; the main
Somaliland inaugurated its embassy to Israel in Jerusalem, drawing domestic and international criticism.
Xenophobic violence claimed several lives as anti-migrant

Violence continued to spread, driven by government-opposition fighting alongside intercommunal conflict and resource-driven clashes; meanwhile, authorities announced elections would take place on 22 December despite insecurity and political turmoil.
The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) massed fighters around El Obeid in North Kordofan, fuelling fears of an imminent assault on the city and prompting the UN to warn about the risk of mass atrocities.
Authorities announced a ban on public rallies ahead of anti-government protests scheduled for 7 July as the main opposition party, Chadema, faced further harassment.
President Museveni’s son and army chief, Gen. Muhoozi Kainerugaba, led an escalating crackdown on dissent while increasingly asserting his political influence.
Political violence left at least one person dead ahead of 13 August general election.

The political crisis escalated after Parliament passed controversial constitutional amendments extending President Mnangagwa’s term in office, deepening divisions within the military and prompting accusations of a “constitutional coup” from the opposition.
Kabul and Islamabad traded cross-border attacks amid worsening acrimony over militancy in Pakistan’s border provinces, raising the rospect of intensifying hostilities.
The former ruling Awami League party stepped up its activity ahead of local elections expected to start in September, while immigration returned as a major flashpoint in relations with India.
Beijing and Washington continued to manage relations amid persistent “irritants” and trade tensions.
Beijing continued its maritime presence in the disputed East China Sea, while the months-long diplomatic rift between Tokyo and Beijing rumbled on.
The ethnic conflict in Manipur state in the far north east continued to simmer amid persistent armed violence, while immigration returned as a major flashpoint in relations with Bangladesh.
Chief Minister Omar Abdullah continued to demand statehood for Jammu & Kashmir (J&K), while ties between India and Pakistan remained severely strained.
Economic discontent fuelled a new wave of student protests, while Jakarta continued developing regional defence ties and military capabilities.
North Korea accelerated the expansion of its navy, as Seoul-Washington nuclear talks drew Pyongyang’s ire; Chinese President Xi Jinping made his first visit to North Korea since 2019.
North Korea accelerated the expansion of its navy, as Seoul-Washington nuclear talks drew Pyongyang’s ire; Chinese President Xi Jinping made his first visit to North Korea since 2019.

The military made uneven progress in its offensives in the north and west amid resistance from armed groups, while a spurt of regional engagements signalled an easing of Myanmar’s years-long diplomatic isolation.

Islamabad and Kabul traded cross-border attacks amid worsening acrimony over militancy in the border provinces, raising the prospect of intensifying hostilities; protests in Pakistan-administered Kashmir turned deadly.
Insecurity persisted in the Bangsamoro region ahead of inaugural parliamentary elections set for September, while the military sustained pressure on Communist militants.
Tensions mounted between Beijing and Manila in the South China Sea (SCS) over a temporary Chinese structure at the contested Scarborough Shoal, while the Philippines deepened regional ties.

The government faced criticism over its use of controversial anti-terror laws and apparent disinterest in expediting the reestablishment of provincial councils, while the investigation into the 2019 Easter bombings advanced.

China maintained its aerial and maritime presence around Taiwan, with the South China Sea increasingly becoming a site of contestation; Taiwan conducted military exercises with an eye on a potential Chinese attack.
Cambodia and Thailand agreed to begin a UN conciliation process to resolve the maritime border dispute, while militant violence in the deep south persisted.

Prime Minister Pashinyan’s ruling Civil Contract party swept to victory in a hotly contested parliamentary election that took on geopolitical hues; Azerbaijan responded positively to the outcome, raising hopes for sustained momentum in the peace process.

Baku appeared to welcome Armenian Prime Minister Pashinyan’s electoral victory as a positive development for the peace process; meanwhile, a U.S. media report alleged that Israel deployed military units to Azerbaijan during the Iran war.

Tensions between Minsk and Kyiv escalated amid fears that Belarus could be dragged deeper into Russia’s war with Ukraine; meanwhile, President Lukashenko and his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping reaffirmed strong bilateral ties.

A court handed down more prison sentences over election protests in October 2025, Tbilisi’s relations with the EU and U.S. remained strained, and the de facto leader of South Ossetia breakaway region resigned and became an adviser to Russian President Putin.

Prime Minister Kurti’s Vetëvendosje party won snap parliamentary polls but fell short of the majority needed to govern alone, likely prolonging the political crisis; meanwhile, NATO announced cuts to peacebuilding troops in the Serb-majority north.
The trial against former security chief Kamchybek Tashiev continued, while Bishkek completed a land swap with Tashkent and won a non-permanent seat on the UN Security Council.

Washington’s haphazard peace efforts between Moscow and Kyiv remained on pause, Russia and Russian-occupied territory faced fuel shortages amid Ukraine’s repeated aerial attacks on logistics and key infrastructure, and Western actors piled on fresh sanctions.
Six police officers went on trial over the death of an individual in custody, while Russian fuel shortages raised concern in Tajikistan, which relies on Russia for over 80% of its fuel imports.
The government and the Kurdistan Workers’ Party (PKK) remained committed to the peace process, as attention turned to parliament to take the next step by adopting new legislation.
Frontline fighting continued, though Russian advances
Tashkent completed a land swap with Bishkek as part of their 2023 border agreement, hosted a regional dialogue and boosted economic ties with Washington.
Tensions eased in Bolivia after President Paz reached a deal with the country’s biggest union and announced a state of exception to remove blockades.

The right-wing candidate Abelardo de la Espriella won by a razor-thin margin in a presidential run-off against left-wing Senator Iván Cepeda, a result that will likely bring an end to President Petro’s “total peace” policy in favour of a hardline security approach.
The U.S. piled on new sanctions, intensifying economic pressure on Cuba and deepening humanitarian harm; faced with a mounting crisis, Havana announced broad economic reforms.

As criminal violence persisted, the government expanded emergency security measures and strengthened ties with Washington; meanwhile, a “recall” effort to remove President Noboa from office gained traction amid growing opposition to his administration.
President Bukele registered his intention to run in 2027 elections, while his government maintained its draconian anti-gang measures and concern over prison conditions continued.

Security forces stepped up operations against gangs in and around the capital Port-au-Prince as the Gang Suppression Force (GSF) began patrols; meanwhile, disputes over electoral rules heightened tensions between the government and electoral authorities.

U.S.-Mexico tensions persisted as Washington renewed pressure over organised crime and extraditions; meanwhile, the football World Cup matches proceeded without major security incidents, though violence continued in high-conflict states.
The government continued its repression and expanded financial oversight as it faced more international pressure.
Right-wing candidate Keiko Fujimori narrowly won a presidential run-off against left-wing rival Roberto Sánchez, who filed an appeal over alleged irregularities.

Twin earthquakes caused devastation, with hundreds of buildings either collapsed or rendered uninhabitable and thousands killed or injured; meanwhile, the U.S. and Venezuela carried out a “joint military operation” against a gang leader, while uncertainty over the political transition persisted.

The opposition denounced the exclusion of candidates from the 2 July legislative elections, while Algeria launched construction of a strategic gas pipeline and deepened economic and security ties with the U.S.
Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a chance to restore regional calm despite Iranian attacks on Bahrain and Kuwait.

Cairo reinforced its role as a mediator by supporting regional efforts around the U.S.-Iran agreement and hosting Gaza ceasefire consultations, while expanding its strategic engagement in the Horn of Africa amid concerns over Red Sea and Nile water security.

Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a narrow pathway – littered with already evident pitfalls – toward a full-fledged settlement.
Despite its formal end, the U.S.-Iran war cast a long shadow over Iraq; meanwhile, two Tehran-backed factions agreed to place weapons under state authority, as hardline groups resisted any such move.

Israel traded blows with Tehran before grudgingly accepting the U.S.-Iran accord, yet its occupation of southern Lebanon looked set to continue following a framework deal with Beirut; the Gaza ceasefire remained stalled, while Israel continued to repress the West Bank.
Ahead of a U.S.-Iran diplomatic breakthrough, Tehran struck U.S. forces in Jordan during tit-for-tat skirmishes in early June.
Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a chance to restore regional calm despite Iranian attacks on Kuwait and Bahrain.

Israeli attacks killed hundreds and threatened to derail U.S.-Iran diplomacy; Hizbollah rejected a late June framework deal between Beirut and Israel, which conditions Israel’s withdrawal on the group’s disarmament.
A U.S. initiative aimed at unifying the country’s rival governments fuelled tensions, while the U.S.-mediated unified budget deal came under increasing strain.
Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a chance to restore regional calm despite skirmishes.
Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a chance to restore regional calm despite skirmishes.
Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a chance to restore regional calm despite skirmishes.
The U.S. urged the Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) to complete its integration with the central government by the end of 2026, while several distinct security challenges came into focus.
Authorities further intensified their crackdown on civil society, while protests over deteriorating socio-economic conditions persisted and
Iran and the U.S. signed a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, offering a chance to restore regional calm despite skirmishes.
A

The Houthis launched their first missile attack on Israel since April and their first maritime strikes in the Gulf of Aden since late 2025; along Yemeni front lines, the group intensified probing attacks on government forces.
